sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments, viticulture research bodies, and the table grape and wine industries.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing grapes are the same: full sun, deep free-draining soil, a sturdy trellis, the right variety and hard winter pruning. Grapes are close cousins of the other Mediterranean fruits, the olive and the fig. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing, pests and the all-important phylloxera rules where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Decide first whether you want a table grape for fresh eating or a wine grape, then whether you want seedless or seeded fruit. For backyard fruit most people want a seedless table grape. Dependable, widely sold types include Crimson Seedless, a bright red, crisp, late-season grape with a thick skin; Flame Seedless, an early red; Thompson Seedless (the Sultana), a pale green grape that is also dried as raisins and is the most grown variety in Australia; Menindee Seedless, a large, early, green grape; and Autumn Royal, a large black seedless that ripens late. Red Globe is a very large red but is seeded. Black Muscat and the white Muscats add a rich muscat flavour. The American slip-skin grapes such as Isabella have a soft skin that slips off the pulp and a musky, foxy taste, and they cope with humidity better than the European wine grapes. Several good Australian seedless varieties came out of CSIRO breeding, including the black Marroo Seedless and the rain-tolerant currant Carina. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available now.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need more than one vine?
sections.body: Cultivated grapevines carry perfect flowers and are self-fertile, so a single vine sets a full crop on its own, with no need for a second vine as a pollinator. That makes grapes one of the easiest backyard fruits to start with: one well-chosen vine on a sunny fence or pergola is enough to fill a fruit bowl.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Grapes need full sun and deep, free-draining soil, and they will not tolerate waterlogging, so avoid heavy clay where water sits and open up clay soils with gypsum before planting. They are not fussy about fertility and crop well in soil that is only moderately rich. A grapevine is a strong, long-lived climber, so give it a solid support: a trellis, a sturdy fence, or a pergola it can cover for years. Plant while the vine is dormant in winter, either bare-rooted or from a pot, into a neutral to slightly acid soil, and check the pH before you plant. For more on timing, see our guide on when to plant.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water new vines through their first summers to get them established, then water bearing vines deeply but less often, easing back as the fruit ripens so the berries concentrate their sugar rather than swell with water. Grapes are light feeders and resent too much nitrogen, which pushes leafy growth at the expense of fruit, delays ripening and can cut the following season's crop. A young vine needs only about 2 to 5 grams of actual nitrogen per vine each week through the growing season, adjusted to how vigorous and green it looks, and a complete fertiliser plus compost in spring suits most backyard vines. Potassium matters because the fruit carries a lot of it away at harvest, so a fertiliser containing potassium (sulphate of potash) supports full bunches, though only very potassium-poor soils need heavy potassium feeding. Keep the soil pH near neutral.

sections.heading: Pruning and training
sections.body: Pruning is the one skill a grapevine really needs. Each winter, while the vine is bare, cut away most of the past season's growth, up to about 90 per cent of it, because grapes fruit only on shoots that grow from one-year-old wood, and a vine left unpruned turns into a tangle that fruits poorly. There are two systems. Spur pruning suits most table and wine varieties, whose buds near the base of each cane are fruitful: you cut last year's canes back to short spurs of two or three buds along a permanent cordon. Cane pruning suits varieties whose basal buds are unfruitful, such as Thompson Seedless (Sultana), where you keep a few long canes of about a dozen buds and remove the rest. Train the vine onto a trellis or pergola so light and air reach the fruit, which also helps keep disease down.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Grapes do not ripen any further once they are picked, so taste before you cut and pick only when the berries are fully coloured, sweet and soft. A grape that is sour on the vine will stay sour in the bowl. In most of Australia backyard grapes ripen from mid or late summer into autumn, earlier in the warm north and later in cool districts. A new vine, whether grafted or on its own roots, usually gives its first real crop in its third growing season, and sometimes a little in the second. Net the vine as the fruit colours, because birds will strip a ripening bunch in days.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named variety from a reputable nursery, either an own-rooted vine or one grafted onto rootstock, and start with healthy, certified planting material. Grape pests and diseases travel on cuttings and vines, so when stock moves between states it should come from an accredited supplier and, for phylloxera, carry the right declaration. Where you live decides whether you should plant own-rooted or grafted vines, which the state sections explain. The table above lists the named varieties for sale now and their prices.

faqs.q: Can I grow a grapevine in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes. A grapevine grows well in a large pot for a few years, which suits a courtyard or balcony and lets you stand it in full sun and train it up a frame. Use a big, deep container with a free-draining mix, water through summer because pots dry out fast, feed in spring, and cut it back hard each winter as you would in the ground. Refresh the mix or pot up every couple of years.

faqs.q: Can I grow grapes from a supermarket grape seed?
faqs.a: It is not worth it. Seedlings do not come true to the parent, they take years longer to bear, and the fruit is often poor; and a seedless grape has no seed to sow at all. Always start from a named vine, a cutting, or a grafted plant, so you know what you are getting and it crops sooner.

A dormant winter vine is very cold hardy and shrugs off frost, which is why grapes grow well in cold districts. The risk is a late frost in spring once the buds have burst, which can burn the soft new shoots and the embryo bunches. In frosty gardens, plant against a sunny wall, prune a little later to delay budburst, and avoid low spots where cold air pools.

Most need a little help against fungal disease, mainly powdery mildew, which dusts the leaves and berries with grey. A few preventative sulphur sprays from when the new shoots emerge in spring usually keep a backyard vine clean, and an open, well-pruned, airy canopy does much of the work. Wetter, more humid gardens need more attention than dry inland ones.

WA.intro: Growing grapes in Western Australia. The south-west has a dry Mediterranean climate that grapes love, WA is one of the parts of Australia still free of grape phylloxera, and table grapes are grown commercially from the Gascoyne down to the South-West.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west of WA has hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters, close to a grape's ideal. The long dry ripening period concentrates sugar and keeps fungal disease low, so the main fungal problem here is powdery mildew rather than the wet-weather diseases of the humid east. The Swan Valley near Perth runs warm to hot and very dry through harvest. Grapes need only a little winter chill to crop, far less than stone fruit, which the south-west supplies easily, and further north the Gascoyne grows table grapes under irrigation in a warm, frost-free climate.

sections.heading: Where grapes grow in WA
sections.body: WA has nine wine regions, including the historic Swan Valley (one of Australia's oldest, with the first vines planted in 1829), Margaret River, the cool Great Southern around Mount Barker and Frankland River, Geographe, Pemberton and Manjimup. Table grapes are a separate, mainly northern story: about 300 commercial vineyards run from Carnarvon and the Gascoyne in the north down through the South-West around Harvey and Donnybrook, and WA grows roughly five per cent of Australia's table grapes.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's heat brings on an early crop. Table grapes from the warm Gascoyne are among the first WA fruit of the season, which nationally starts about November, while the Swan Valley and the southern districts ripen through summer into autumn.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Dry summers keep WA's grape disease pressure low, with powdery mildew the main fungal watch and far less downy mildew than the humid east. The fruit fly here is the Mediterranean fruit fly (Medfly), established across most of WA, which treats grapes as a favoured host, so net or bag ripening bunches; Medfly is, however, absent from the Ord River area at Kununurra. Garden weevil, a pest of WA vineyards, notches the leaves and can ringbark young vines, and birds take ripe fruit.

sections.heading: Phylloxera and bringing vines into WA
sections.body: WA is free of grape phylloxera, the root-feeding insect that kills vines in the eastern states, and it is a declared pest the state works hard to keep out. Because WA is free of it, you can plant own-rooted grapevines here without the grafted resistant rootstock that infested eastern zones require. The trade-off is strict quarantine: grapevines coming into WA must be a permitted organism on the WA Organism List and usually need treatment and plant-health certification, so many interstate nurseries will not post vines here. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Which grape varieties suit Perth and the south-west?
faqs.a: Almost any table grape does well in the dry south-west. For an easy backyard vine, seedless types such as Crimson Seedless, Flame Seedless, Thompson Seedless (Sultana) and the black Marroo Seedless are reliable, and the warm Swan District will ripen even late kinds. In the hot Gascoyne, lean on the earliest types and keep the water up while the bunches fill.

faqs.q: Can grapes cope with Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes. Grapes handle free-draining Perth sand better than most fruit, as long as you improve it with compost and a thick mulch, water through summer, and feed a little and often, because sandy soils dry out and lose nutrients quickly. The sharp drainage actually suits grapes, which hate having wet feet.

QLD.intro: Growing grapes in Queensland. Grapes prefer a dry, Mediterranean-style climate, so in Queensland they do best in the dry inland and on the elevated Granite Belt rather than the humid coast or the tropics. The dry inland grows some of the earliest table grapes in the country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Grapes dislike summer humidity, which feeds fungal disease, so the warm wet coast and the tropics are hard country for them and need an overhead trellis, netting and regular spraying. The dependable areas are dry and inland, or high and cool. The Granite Belt around Stanthorpe sits above 800 metres with cold winter nights and genuine chill, which suits wine grapes, while the hot, dry inland districts grow table grapes under irrigation.

sections.heading: Where grapes grow in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's table grapes come from the warm, dry inland: St George, Mundubbera and Emerald are the main districts. The Granite Belt, centred on Stanthorpe, is the state's best-known wine region, high and cool enough for grapes that need real winter chill.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: The hot inland opens Queensland's table-grape season early, from about November, putting Emerald and St George among the first grapes on the Australian market, ahead of the southern states. The cooler Granite Belt ripens later, through summer and into early autumn.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Humidity is the main enemy. Downy mildew, powdery mildew, black spot and botrytis bunch rot all build up in warm, wet weather, so coastal and tropical vines need an open canopy and a spray program. Queensland fruit fly is endemic and uses grapes as a host, so ripening bunches must be netted or bagged, and in the tropics fruit-piercing moths and birds add to the pressure.

sections.heading: Phylloxera in QLD
sections.body: Queensland is not known to carry grape phylloxera and is treated as a phylloxera exclusion zone, with a dedicated zone protecting the main inland grape districts, so own-rooted vines are fine here. The state guards that status: do not bring in vines, cuttings or used vineyard gear from the infested zones of Victoria and New South Wales, and buy from accredited, certified sources.

faqs.q: Which grapes suit a humid Brisbane or coastal garden?
faqs.a: Near the coast, lean toward the tougher American slip-skin grapes such as Isabella, and the muscadine grapes sold for warm climates, which shrug off disease far better than the thin-skinned European table grapes. Train them on an open overhead frame for airflow and net against birds and fruit fly. The mainstream seedless table grapes are better kept for the dry inland and the Granite Belt.

faqs.q: Are home-grown grapes worth it in the Queensland tropics?
faqs.a: They can be, but go in with eyes open. A few varieties do fruit in the wet tropics on an overhead trellis with netting and frequent sprays, and a vine doubles as summer shade, but yields are modest and the wet season can spoil a ripening crop. Many tropical gardeners grow grapes as much for the leafy pergola as for the fruit.

NSW.intro: Growing grapes in New South Wales. NSW grows grapes in almost every climate, from the hot irrigated Riverina and the historic Hunter Valley to the cool tablelands, and parts of the state sit inside phylloxera zones that set the rules for planting.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: NSW spans warm and cool grape country. The hot inland Riverina around Griffith grows table and bulk wine grapes, but only with irrigation, because natural rainfall is low. The Hunter Valley, Australia's oldest wine region, is warm and humid, and rain near harvest makes downy mildew and bunch rot the big risks. Cooler, higher districts such as Orange, Mudgee and Tumbarumba ripen later and suit cool-climate wine varieties.

sections.heading: Where grapes grow in NSW
sections.body: The Riverina, centred on Griffith, is the state's largest grape district and a major source of Australian table grapes. The Hunter Valley north of Sydney is the historic heart of NSW wine, and cooler regions including Mudgee, Orange and Tumbarumba grow wine grapes at higher, cooler altitudes.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Warm districts pick first: the Hunter's heat brings on one of the earliest harvests in the country, and Riverina table grapes ripen through summer into autumn, in step with the national November to May season. The cool tableland districts finish later.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: In the humid Hunter and on the warm coast, downy mildew and botrytis bunch rot are the main threats, worse in wet springs and near harvest, so airflow and a spray program matter. Queensland fruit fly is a host risk in the warmer parts of the state, and birds take ripening fruit everywhere.

sections.heading: Phylloxera in NSW
sections.body: Grape phylloxera is established in defined parts of NSW, with infested zones around Albury and Corowa on the Victorian border and in the Sydney region. If you garden in or near an infested zone, plant grafted vines on resistant rootstock and never move vines, cuttings, grapes or soil out of the zone; elsewhere in the state, buy certified vines and help keep the pest from spreading.

faqs.q: Which grapes suit the cool New South Wales tablelands?
faqs.a: Higher, colder districts give grapes the cool nights that build flavour, and they suit wine varieties and the firmer, later table grapes rather than the heat-loving early kinds. Pick a warm, sunny, north-facing spot, accept a later harvest than the warm inland, and watch for spring frost on the new shoots.

faqs.q: Can I grow grapes near the coast in Sydney?
faqs.a: Yes, with a little planning. Coastal humidity favours disease, so give the vine an open, airy spot or an overhead frame, prune for good airflow, and keep up preventative sprays. Choose tougher, disease-tolerant or slip-skin types over the thinnest-skinned table grapes, and net the bunches against birds and fruit fly.

VIC.intro: Growing grapes in Victoria. Victoria grows more grapes than any other state, from the vast irrigated table-grape blocks of Sunraysia to the cool wine districts of the south, and it also carries Australia's most significant grape phylloxera zones, which shape how you plant.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria has two grape climates. The hot, dry, irrigated north-west along the Murray, around Mildura, is the engine room of Australian grape growing and the main home of table grapes, where irrigation does the watering the low rainfall cannot. The cooler south and the elevated north-east, from the Yarra Valley and the alpine King Valley to Rutherglen, ripen wine grapes more slowly, which growers prize for quality but which also brings more disease pressure in wet seasons.

sections.heading: Where grapes grow in VIC
sections.body: Sunraysia, around Mildura and Robinvale on the Murray, is one of Australia's largest grape-growing areas and the leading source of table grapes, watered entirely by irrigation. The cool south grows wine grapes in the Yarra Valley, the Mornington Peninsula and Macedon; the warm central districts in the Goulburn Valley around Nagambie and at Heathcote; and the north-east in the King Valley, Rutherglen and Glenrowan, famous for fortified Muscat.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Sunraysia's table grapes ripen across the heart of the national season, peaking in February and March, and supply much of the country's fresh grapes. The cool southern and north-eastern wine districts ripen later, often into April and May.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: The wetter, cooler south sees more powdery mildew, downy mildew and botrytis bunch rot than the dry north. Light brown apple moth, a native pest, chews the shoots and bunches and opens the way for bunch rot, and birds are a constant. Across the state, the overriding biosecurity issue is phylloxera, covered below.

sections.heading: Phylloxera in Victoria
sections.body: Victoria carries Australia's main grape phylloxera infestations, in infested zones including the North East (which covers Rutherglen and the King Valley), Nagambie, the Maroondah zone in the Yarra Valley (where the pest was found in 2006), Mooroopna, Upton and Whitebridge. Because nearly all Australian vines grow on their own roots, which phylloxera kills, growers in these zones must plant on resistant rootstock and follow strict hygiene: no moving soil, vines, cuttings, grapes or equipment out of an infested zone. If you garden in one of these areas, buy grafted vines and check the current zone boundaries before you plant.

faqs.q: Which grapes suit cool southern Victoria?
faqs.a: The cool south ripens grapes slowly, which suits wine varieties and the earlier, firmer table grapes more than the latest heat-lovers. Give the vine the warmest, sunniest spot you have, ideally against a north-facing wall, choose an early-ripening seedless type for fresh eating, and expect a later harvest than the warm Murray districts.

faqs.q: Why is moving grapevines around Victoria so tightly controlled?
faqs.a: Because phylloxera, once it reaches a vineyard, kills own-rooted vines and cannot be cured, and Victoria has the country's main infested areas. Soil, cuttings, machinery and even dirty boots can carry it, so the zone rules stop material moving out of infested districts. For a home gardener it means buying clean, certified vines and not bringing home cuttings or soil from an infested area.
